# 大纲规划任务

项目名: PLFM_RADAR
讲义目录: PLFM_RADAR-tutorial/
模式: full
当前 HEAD: 749bd0f86a07a28a86347d8ce9c141e601057c40
代码永久链接 base: https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/blob/749bd0f86a07a28a86347d8ce9c141e601057c40/
用户关注点: （无）


---

## 执行要求

请按照 planner prompt 中的规则执行。

你需要输出：

- 一个完整 manifest JSON。
- full 模式下，所有讲义 `action` 为 `"new"`。
- incremental 模式下，根据 diff 判断每篇讲义的 `action`。

不要写任何 Markdown 讲义文件。

不要输出 JSON 以外的任何文字。

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	[![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR?style=social)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/stargazers)
2	[![GitHub forks](https://img.shields.io/github/forks/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR?style=social)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/network/members)
3	[![GitHub watchers](https://img.shields.io/github/watchers/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR?style=social)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/watchers)
4	[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
5	[![Hardware: CERN-OHL-P](https://img.shields.io/badge/Hardware-CERN--OHL--P-blue.svg)](https://ohwr.org/cern_ohl_p_v2.txt)
6	
7	# AERIS-10: Open Source Pulse Linear Frequency Modulated Phased Array Radar
8	
9	[![Hardware: CERN-OHL-P](https://img.shields.io/badge/Hardware-CERN--OHL--P-blue.svg)](https://ohwr.org/cern_ohl_p_v2.txt)
10	[![Software: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/Software-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
11	[![Status: Alpha](https://img.shields.io/badge/Status-Alpha-orange)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR)
12	[![Features: Work in Progress](https://img.shields.io/badge/Features-Work_in_Progress-yellow)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/issues)
13	[![Frequency: 10.5GHz](https://img.shields.io/badge/Frequency-10.5GHz-blue)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR)
14	[![PRs Welcome](https://img.shields.io/badge/PRs-welcome-brightgreen.svg)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/pulls)
15	
16	
17	AERIS-10 is an open-source, low-cost 10.5 GHz phased array radar system featuring Pulse Linear Frequency Modulated (LFM) modulation. Available in two versions (3km and 20km range), it's designed for researchers, drone developers, and serious SDR enthusiasts who want to explore and experiment with phased array radar technology.
18	
19	![AERIS-10 Antenna Array](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/Antenna_Array.jpg)
20	
21	## 📡 Overview
22	
23	The AERIS-10 project aims to democratize radar technology by providing a fully open-source, modular, and hackable radar system. Whether you're a university researcher, a drone startup, or an advanced maker, AERIS-10 offers a platform for experimenting with beamforming, pulse compression, Doppler processing, and target tracking.
24	
25	## 🔬 Key Features
26	
27	- **Open Source Hardware & Software** - Complete schematics, PCB layouts, firmware, and software available
28	- **Dual Version Availability**:
29	  - **AERIS-10N (Nexus)**: 3km range with 8x16 patch antenna array
30	  - **AERIS-10E (Extended)**: 20km range with 32x16 dielectric-filled slotted waveguide array
31	- **Full Electronic Beam Steering** - ±45° electronic steering in elevation and azimuth
32	- **Advanced Signal Processing** - On-board FPGA handles pulse compression, Doppler FFT, MTI, and CFAR
33	- **Python GUI** - User-friendly interface with map integration
34	- **GPS/IMU Integration** - Real-time position and attitude correction
35	- **Modular Design** - Separate power management, frequency synthesis, and RF boards
36	
37	## 🏗️ System Architecture
38	
39	![AERIS-10 System Diagram](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/RADAR_V6_V2.png)
40	
41	### Hardware Components
42	
43	The AERIS-10 main sub-systems are:
44	
45	- **Power Management Board** - Supplies all necessary voltage levels to the electronics components with proper filtering and sequencing (sequencing ensured by the microcontroller)
46	
47	- **Frequency Synthesizer Board** - Uses a high-performance Low Jitter Clock Generator (AD9523-1) that supplies phase-aligned clock references for:
48	  - RX and TX Frequency Synthesizers (ADF4382)
49	  - DAC
50	  - ADC
51	  - FPGA
52	
53	- **Main Board** containing:
54	  - **DAC** - Generates the RADAR Chirps
55	  - **2x Microwave Mixers (LTC5552)** - For up-conversion and IF-down-conversion
56	  - **4x 4-Channel Phase Shifters (ADAR1000)** - For RX and TX chain beamforming
57	  - **16x Front End Chips (ADTR1107)** - Used for both Low Noise Amplifying (RX) and Power Amplifying (TX) stages
58	  - **XC7A50T FPGA** - Handles RADAR Signal Processing on the upstream FTG256 board:
59	    - PLFM Chirps generation via the DAC
60	    - Raw ADC data read
61	    - Hybrid Automatic Gain Control (AGC) — cross-layer FPGA/STM32/GUI loop
62	    - I/Q Baseband Down-Conversion
63	    - Decimation
64	    - Filtering
65	    - Forward FFT
66	    - Pulse Compression
67	    - Doppler, MTI and CFAR processing
68	    - USB Interface
69	  - **STM32F746xx Microcontroller** - Used for:
70	    - Power-up and power-down sequencing (see Power Management Excel File)
71	    - FPGA communication
72	    - Setup and Interface with:
73	      - Clock Generator (AD9523-1)
74	      - 2x Frequency Synthesizers (ADF4382)
75	      - 4x 4-Channel Phase Shifters (ADAR1000) for RADAR pulse sequencing
76	      - 2x ADS7830 8-channel I²C ADCs (Main Board, U88 @ 0x48 / U89 @ 0x4A) for 16x Idq measurement, one per PA channel, each sensed through a 5 mΩ shunt on the PA board and an INA241A3 current-sense amplifier (x50) on the Main Board
77	      - 2x DAC5578 8-channel I²C DACs (Main Board, U7 @ 0x48 / U69 @ 0x49) for 16x Vg control, one per PA channel; closed-loop calibrated at boot to the target Idq
78	      - GPS module (UM982) for GUI map centering and per-detection position tagging
79	      - GY-85 IMU for pitch/roll correction of target coordinates
80	      - BMP180 Barometer
81	      - Stepper Motor
82	      - 1x ADS7830 8-channel I²C ADC (Main Board, U10) reading 8 thermistors for thermal monitoring; a single GPIO (EN_DIS_COOLING) switches the cooling fans on when any channel exceeds the threshold
83	      - RF switches
84	
85	- **16x Power Amplifier Boards** - Used only for AERIS-10E version, featuring 10Watt QPA2962 GaN amplifier for extended range
86	
87	- **Antenna Arrays**:
88	  - **AERIS-10N (Nexus)** - 8x16 patch antenna array
89	  - **AERIS-10X (Extended)** - 32x16 dielectric-filled slotted waveguide antenna array
90	
91	- **Miscellaneous Components**:
92	  - Slip-Ring
93	  - Stepper Motor and drivers
94	  - Cooling Fans
95	  - Enclosure
96	
97	### Processing Pipeline
98	
99	1. **Waveform Generation** - DAC creates LFM chirps
100	2. **Up/Down Conversion** - LTC5552 mixers handle frequency translation
101	3. **Beam Steering** - ADAR1000 phase shifters control 16 elements
102	4. **Signal Processing (FPGA)**:
103	   - Raw ADC data capture
104	   - I/Q baseband down-conversion
105	   - Decimation & filtering (CIC/FIR)
106	   - Pulse compression
107	   - Doppler FFT processing
108	   - MTI & CFAR detection
109	5. **System Management (STM32)**:
110	   - Power sequencing
111	   - Peripheral configuration
112	   - GPS/IMU integration
113	   - Stepper motor control
114	6. **Visualization (Python GUI)**:
115	   - Real-time target plotting
116	   - Map integration
117	   - Radar control interface
118	
119	![AERIS-10 Dashboard](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/GUI_V6.gif)
120	<!-- V6 GIF removed — V6 is deprecated. V65 Tk and V7 PyQt6 are the active GUIs. -->
121	
122	## 📊 Technical Specifications
123	
124	| Parameter | AERIS-10N (Nexus) | AERIS-10X (Extended) |
125	|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
126	| **Frequency** | 10.5 GHz | 10.5 GHz |
127	| **Max Range** | 3 km | 20 km |
128	| **Antenna** | 8x16 Patch Array | 32x16 Slotted Waveguide |
129	| **Beam Steering** | Electronic (±45°) | Electronic (±45°) |
130	| **Mechanical Scan** | 360° (stepper motor) | 360° (stepper motor) |
131	| **Output Power** | ~1W×16 | 10W×16 (GaN amplifier) |
132	| **Processing** | FPGA + STM32 | FPGA + STM32 |
133	
134	## 🚀 Getting Started
135	
136	### 🧹 Repository File Placement Policy
137	
138	To keep the repository root clean and make artifacts easy to find, place generated files in the following locations:
139	
140	- **Published reports (tracked, GitHub Pages):** `docs/`
141	  - Example: `docs/AERIS_Simulation_Report_v2.pdf`
142	- **Simulation-generated outputs (local, gitignored):** `5_Simulations/generated/`
143	  - Plots, scenario outputs, temporary analysis directories
144	- **FPGA/Vivado generated artifacts (local, gitignored):** `9_Firmware/9_2_FPGA/reports/`
145	  - VCD/VVP dumps, temporary CSVs, local report snapshots
146	- **Reusable FPGA automation scripts (tracked):** `9_Firmware/9_2_FPGA/scripts/`
147	  - TCL flows, helper scripts used by build/bring-up
148	
149	**Do not leave generated artifacts in the repository root.**
150	
151	### Prerequisites
152	
153	- Basic understanding of radar principles
154	- Experience with PCB assembly (for hardware build)
155	- Python 3.8+ for the GUI software
156	- FPGA development tools (Vivado) for signal processing modifications
157	
158	### Hardware Assembly
159	
160	1. **Order PCBs**: Production outputs are under `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout/4_7_Production Files`
161	2. **Source Components**: BOM/CPL files are co-located under `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout/4_7_Production Files`
162	3. **Assembly**: Use the schematics in `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout/4_6_Schematics` together with the production outputs above; a standalone assembly guide is not currently tracked
163	4. **Antenna**: Choose appropriate array files for your target variant
164	5. **Enclosure**: Mechanical drawings currently live in `/8_Utils/Mechanical_Drawings`
165	
166	## 📜 License
167	
168	This project is open-source but uses **different licenses for hardware and software** to ensure proper legal coverage.
169	
170	### Hardware Documentation
171	The hardware design files—including:
172	- Schematics and PCB layouts (in `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout`)
173	- Bill of Materials (BOM) files
174	- Gerber files and manufacturing outputs
175	- Mechanical drawings and enclosure designs
176	
177	are licensed under the **CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 – Permissive (CERN-OHL-P)** .
178	
179	This is a hardware-specific license that:
180	- ✅ Clearly defines "Hardware," "Documentation," and "Products"
181	- ✅ Includes explicit patent protection for contributors and users
182	- ✅ Provides stronger liability limitations (important for high-power RF)
183	- ✅ Aligns with professional open-hardware standards (CERN, OSHWA)
184	
185	You may use, modify, and sell products based on these designs, provided you:
186	- Maintain the original copyright notices
187	- Distribute any modified designs under the same license
188	- Make your modifications available in Source format
189	
190	### Software and Firmware
191	The software components—including:
192	- FPGA code (VHDL/Verilog in `/9_Firmware`)
193	- Microcontroller firmware (STM32)
194	- Python GUI and utilities
195	
196	remain under the **MIT License** for maximum flexibility.
197	
198	### Full License Texts
199	- The complete CERN-OHL-P license text is in the `LICENSE` file
200	- MIT license terms apply to software where not otherwise specified
201	
202	### Why This Change?
203	Originally, the entire project used the MIT license. The community (special thanks to gmaynez!) pointed out that MIT lacks legal protections needed for physical hardware. The switch to CERN-OHL-P ensures the project is properly protected while maintaining the same permissive spirit.
204	
205	## 📚 Documentation
206	
207	Comprehensive documentation is available in the `/docs` folder and served via GitHub Pages at [https://NawfalMotii79.github.io/PLFM_RADAR/docs/](https://NawfalMotii79.github.io/PLFM_RADAR/docs/):
208	
209	- [System Architecture](/docs/architecture.html)
210	- [Implementation Log](/docs/implementation-log.html)
211	- [Hardware Bring-Up Guide](/docs/bring-up.html)
212	- [Test Reports](/docs/reports.html)
213	- [Release Notes](/docs/release-notes.html)
214	
215	## 🤝 Contributing
216	
217	We welcome contributions! Please see our [Contributing Guidelines](/CONTRIBUTING.md) for details on repo layout, branch workflow, and basic PR checks.
218	
219	Areas where help is especially appreciated:
220	- **RF Engineers**: Review designs, optimize antenna performance
221	- **FPGA Developers**: Optimize signal processing pipeline
222	- **Software Developers**: Enhance Python GUI and SDK
223	- **Beta Testers**: University researchers, drone startups, advanced makers
224	
225	## 📞 Contact & Collaboration
226	
227	I welcome serious inquiries from researchers, engineers, and potential collaborators. However, due to the high volume of interest in this project, please understand that I cannot guarantee a response to every message.
228	
229	- **Technical questions or bug reports**: Please [open a GitHub issue](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/issues) so the whole community can benefit from the discussion.
230	- **Collaboration, licensing, or business inquiries**: 📧 nawfal.motii.33 [at] gmail [dot] com
231	
232	## 💰 Sponsors
233	
234	![PCBWay Sponsor Logo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/PCBWAY.jpg)
235	
236	---
237	
238	**Star ⭐ this repository if you're interested in open-source radar technology!**
239	
240	*Note: This is an active development project. Some features are still in progress. Check the issues page for known limitations and upcoming features.*
241	
242	## 19,000 stars – Thank you
243	
244	This project started in a small workshop in Morocco. Today, 19,000 engineers on GitHub have starred it.
245	
246	I am genuinely humbled.
247	
248	**What this tells me:**
249	
250	- Open source radar matters
251	- Affordable sensing is needed
252	- Engineers want to build, not just buy
253	
254	Thank you to everyone who starred, forked, opened issues, submitted PRs, and shared this project.
255	
256	The work continues.
257	
258	**Nawfal Motii**
259	ABAC INDUSTRY
260	(http://www.abacindustry.com)

# AERIS-10: Open Source Pulse Linear Frequency Modulated Phased Array Radar

[![Hardware: CERN-OHL-P](https://img.shields.io/badge/Hardware-CERN--OHL--P-blue.svg)](https://ohwr.org/cern_ohl_p_v2.txt)
[![Software: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/Software-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
[![Status: Alpha](https://img.shields.io/badge/Status-Alpha-orange)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR)
[![Features: Work in Progress](https://img.shields.io/badge/Features-Work_in_Progress-yellow)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/issues)
[![Frequency: 10.5GHz](https://img.shields.io/badge/Frequency-10.5GHz-blue)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR)
[![PRs Welcome](https://img.shields.io/badge/PRs-welcome-brightgreen.svg)](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/pulls)


AERIS-10 is an open-source, low-cost 10.5 GHz phased array radar system featuring Pulse Linear Frequency Modulated (LFM) modulation. Available in two versions (3km and 20km range), it's designed for researchers, drone developers, and serious SDR enthusiasts who want to explore and experiment with phased array radar technology.

![AERIS-10 Antenna Array](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/Antenna_Array.jpg)

## 📡 Overview

The AERIS-10 project aims to democratize radar technology by providing a fully open-source, modular, and hackable radar system. Whether you're a university researcher, a drone startup, or an advanced maker, AERIS-10 offers a platform for experimenting with beamforming, pulse compression, Doppler processing, and target tracking.

## 🔬 Key Features

- **Open Source Hardware & Software** - Complete schematics, PCB layouts, firmware, and software available
- **Dual Version Availability**:
  - **AERIS-10N (Nexus)**: 3km range with 8x16 patch antenna array
  - **AERIS-10E (Extended)**: 20km range with 32x16 dielectric-filled slotted waveguide array
- **Full Electronic Beam Steering** - ±45° electronic steering in elevation and azimuth
- **Advanced Signal Processing** - On-board FPGA handles pulse compression, Doppler FFT, MTI, and CFAR
- **Python GUI** - User-friendly interface with map integration
- **GPS/IMU Integration** - Real-time position and attitude correction
- **Modular Design** - Separate power management, frequency synthesis, and RF boards

## 🏗️ System Architecture

![AERIS-10 System Diagram](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/RADAR_V6_V2.png)

### Hardware Components

The AERIS-10 main sub-systems are:

- **Power Management Board** - Supplies all necessary voltage levels to the electronics components with proper filtering and sequencing (sequencing ensured by the microcontroller)

- **Frequency Synthesizer Board** - Uses a high-performance Low Jitter Clock Generator (AD9523-1) that supplies phase-aligned clock references for:
  - RX and TX Frequency Synthesizers (ADF4382)
  - DAC
  - ADC
  - FPGA

- **Main Board** containing:
  - **DAC** - Generates the RADAR Chirps
  - **2x Microwave Mixers (LTC5552)** - For up-conversion and IF-down-conversion
  - **4x 4-Channel Phase Shifters (ADAR1000)** - For RX and TX chain beamforming
  - **16x Front End Chips (ADTR1107)** - Used for both Low Noise Amplifying (RX) and Power Amplifying (TX) stages
  - **XC7A50T FPGA** - Handles RADAR Signal Processing on the upstream FTG256 board:
    - PLFM Chirps generation via the DAC
    - Raw ADC data read
    - Hybrid Automatic Gain Control (AGC) — cross-layer FPGA/STM32/GUI loop
    - I/Q Baseband Down-Conversion
    - Decimation
    - Filtering
    - Forward FFT
    - Pulse Compression
    - Doppler, MTI and CFAR processing
    - USB Interface
  - **STM32F746xx Microcontroller** - Used for:
    - Power-up and power-down sequencing (see Power Management Excel File)
    - FPGA communication
    - Setup and Interface with:
      - Clock Generator (AD9523-1)
      - 2x Frequency Synthesizers (ADF4382)
      - 4x 4-Channel Phase Shifters (ADAR1000) for RADAR pulse sequencing
      - 2x ADS7830 8-channel I²C ADCs (Main Board, U88 @ 0x48 / U89 @ 0x4A) for 16x Idq measurement, one per PA channel, each sensed through a 5 mΩ shunt on the PA board and an INA241A3 current-sense amplifier (x50) on the Main Board
      - 2x DAC5578 8-channel I²C DACs (Main Board, U7 @ 0x48 / U69 @ 0x49) for 16x Vg control, one per PA channel; closed-loop calibrated at boot to the target Idq
      - GPS module (UM982) for GUI map centering and per-detection position tagging
      - GY-85 IMU for pitch/roll correction of target coordinates
      - BMP180 Barometer
      - Stepper Motor
      - 1x ADS7830 8-channel I²C ADC (Main Board, U10) reading 8 thermistors for thermal monitoring; a single GPIO (EN_DIS_COOLING) switches the cooling fans on when any channel exceeds the threshold
      - RF switches

- **16x Power Amplifier Boards** - Used only for AERIS-10E version, featuring 10Watt QPA2962 GaN amplifier for extended range

- **Antenna Arrays**:
  - **AERIS-10N (Nexus)** - 8x16 patch antenna array
  - **AERIS-10X (Extended)** - 32x16 dielectric-filled slotted waveguide antenna array

- **Miscellaneous Components**:
  - Slip-Ring
  - Stepper Motor and drivers
  - Cooling Fans
  - Enclosure

### Processing Pipeline

1. **Waveform Generation** - DAC creates LFM chirps
2. **Up/Down Conversion** - LTC5552 mixers handle frequency translation
3. **Beam Steering** - ADAR1000 phase shifters control 16 elements
4. **Signal Processing (FPGA)**:
   - Raw ADC data capture
   - I/Q baseband down-conversion
   - Decimation & filtering (CIC/FIR)
   - Pulse compression
   - Doppler FFT processing
   - MTI & CFAR detection
5. **System Management (STM32)**:
   - Power sequencing
   - Peripheral configuration
   - GPS/IMU integration
   - Stepper motor control
6. **Visualization (Python GUI)**:
   - Real-time target plotting
   - Map integration
   - Radar control interface

![AERIS-10 Dashboard](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/GUI_V6.gif)
<!-- V6 GIF removed — V6 is deprecated. V65 Tk and V7 PyQt6 are the active GUIs. -->

## 📊 Technical Specifications

| Parameter | AERIS-10N (Nexus) | AERIS-10X (Extended) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Frequency** | 10.5 GHz | 10.5 GHz |
| **Max Range** | 3 km | 20 km |
| **Antenna** | 8x16 Patch Array | 32x16 Slotted Waveguide |
| **Beam Steering** | Electronic (±45°) | Electronic (±45°) |
| **Mechanical Scan** | 360° (stepper motor) | 360° (stepper motor) |
| **Output Power** | ~1W×16 | 10W×16 (GaN amplifier) |
| **Processing** | FPGA + STM32 | FPGA + STM32 |

## 🚀 Getting Started

### 🧹 Repository File Placement Policy

To keep the repository root clean and make artifacts easy to find, place generated files in the following locations:

- **Published reports (tracked, GitHub Pages):** `docs/`
  - Example: `docs/AERIS_Simulation_Report_v2.pdf`
- **Simulation-generated outputs (local, gitignored):** `5_Simulations/generated/`
  - Plots, scenario outputs, temporary analysis directories
- **FPGA/Vivado generated artifacts (local, gitignored):** `9_Firmware/9_2_FPGA/reports/`
  - VCD/VVP dumps, temporary CSVs, local report snapshots
- **Reusable FPGA automation scripts (tracked):** `9_Firmware/9_2_FPGA/scripts/`
  - TCL flows, helper scripts used by build/bring-up

**Do not leave generated artifacts in the repository root.**

### Prerequisites

- Basic understanding of radar principles
- Experience with PCB assembly (for hardware build)
- Python 3.8+ for the GUI software
- FPGA development tools (Vivado) for signal processing modifications

### Hardware Assembly

1. **Order PCBs**: Production outputs are under `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout/4_7_Production Files`
2. **Source Components**: BOM/CPL files are co-located under `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout/4_7_Production Files`
3. **Assembly**: Use the schematics in `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout/4_6_Schematics` together with the production outputs above; a standalone assembly guide is not currently tracked
4. **Antenna**: Choose appropriate array files for your target variant
5. **Enclosure**: Mechanical drawings currently live in `/8_Utils/Mechanical_Drawings`

## 📜 License

This project is open-source but uses **different licenses for hardware and software** to ensure proper legal coverage.

### Hardware Documentation
The hardware design files—including:
- Schematics and PCB layouts (in `/4_Schematics and Boards Layout`)
- Bill of Materials (BOM) files
- Gerber files and manufacturing outputs
- Mechanical drawings and enclosure designs

are licensed under the **CERN Open Hardware Licence Version 2 – Permissive (CERN-OHL-P)** .

This is a hardware-specific license that:
- ✅ Clearly defines "Hardware," "Documentation," and "Products"
- ✅ Includes explicit patent protection for contributors and users
- ✅ Provides stronger liability limitations (important for high-power RF)
- ✅ Aligns with professional open-hardware standards (CERN, OSHWA)

You may use, modify, and sell products based on these designs, provided you:
- Maintain the original copyright notices
- Distribute any modified designs under the same license
- Make your modifications available in Source format

### Software and Firmware
The software components—including:
- FPGA code (VHDL/Verilog in `/9_Firmware`)
- Microcontroller firmware (STM32)
- Python GUI and utilities

remain under the **MIT License** for maximum flexibility.

### Full License Texts
- The complete CERN-OHL-P license text is in the `LICENSE` file
- MIT license terms apply to software where not otherwise specified

### Why This Change?
Originally, the entire project used the MIT license. The community (special thanks to gmaynez!) pointed out that MIT lacks legal protections needed for physical hardware. The switch to CERN-OHL-P ensures the project is properly protected while maintaining the same permissive spirit.

## 📚 Documentation

Comprehensive documentation is available in the `/docs` folder and served via GitHub Pages at [https://NawfalMotii79.github.io/PLFM_RADAR/docs/](https://NawfalMotii79.github.io/PLFM_RADAR/docs/):

- [System Architecture](/docs/architecture.html)
- [Implementation Log](/docs/implementation-log.html)
- [Hardware Bring-Up Guide](/docs/bring-up.html)
- [Test Reports](/docs/reports.html)
- [Release Notes](/docs/release-notes.html)

## 🤝 Contributing

We welcome contributions! Please see our [Contributing Guidelines](/CONTRIBUTING.md) for details on repo layout, branch workflow, and basic PR checks.

Areas where help is especially appreciated:
- **RF Engineers**: Review designs, optimize antenna performance
- **FPGA Developers**: Optimize signal processing pipeline
- **Software Developers**: Enhance Python GUI and SDK
- **Beta Testers**: University researchers, drone startups, advanced makers

## 📞 Contact & Collaboration

I welcome serious inquiries from researchers, engineers, and potential collaborators. However, due to the high volume of interest in this project, please understand that I cannot guarantee a response to every message.

- **Technical questions or bug reports**: Please [open a GitHub issue](https://github.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/issues) so the whole community can benefit from the discussion.
- **Collaboration, licensing, or business inquiries**: 📧 nawfal.motii.33 [at] gmail [dot] com

## 💰 Sponsors

![PCBWay Sponsor Logo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NawfalMotii79/PLFM_RADAR/main/8_Utils/PCBWAY.jpg)

---

**Star ⭐ this repository if you're interested in open-source radar technology!**

*Note: This is an active development project. Some features are still in progress. Check the issues page for known limitations and upcoming features.*

## 19,000 stars – Thank you

This project started in a small workshop in Morocco. Today, 19,000 engineers on GitHub have starred it.

I am genuinely humbled.

**What this tells me:**

- Open source radar matters
- Affordable sensing is needed
- Engineers want to build, not just buy

Thank you to everyone who starred, forked, opened issues, submitted PRs, and shared this project.

The work continues.

**Nawfal Motii**

[project]
name = "aeris-10-radar"
version = "1.0.0"
description = "AERIS-10 FMCW Radar Platform — host software & FPGA cosim tools"
requires-python = ">=3.12"

# Runtime dependencies intentionally empty — GUI deps are optional and
# listed in requirements_*.txt files for local installs.
dependencies = []

[dependency-groups]
dev = [
    "ruff>=0.5",
    "pytest>=8",
    "numpy>=1.26",
    "h5py>=3.10",
]

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Ruff configuration
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
[tool.ruff]
target-version = "py312"
line-length = 100

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = [
    "E",    # pycodestyle errors
    "F",    # pyflakes (unused imports, undefined names, duplicate keys, assert-tuple)
    "B",    # flake8-bugbear (mutable defaults, unreachable code, raise-without-from)
    "RUF",  # ruff-specific (unused noqa, ambiguous chars, implicit Optional)
    "SIM",  # flake8-simplify (dead branches, collapsible ifs, unnecessary pass)
    "PIE",  # flake8-pie (no-op expressions, unnecessary spread)
    "T20",  # flake8-print (stray print() calls — LLMs leave debug prints)
    "ARG",  # flake8-unused-arguments (LLMs generate params they never use)
    "ERA",  # eradicate (commented-out code — LLMs leave "alternatives" as comments)
    "A",    # flake8-builtins (LLMs shadow id, type, list, dict, input, map)
    "BLE",  # flake8-blind-except (bare except / overly broad except)
    "RET",  # flake8-return (unreachable code after return, unnecessary else-after-return)
    "ISC",  # flake8-implicit-str-concat (missing comma in list of strings)
    "TCH",  # flake8-type-checking (imports only used in type hints — move behind TYPE_CHECKING)
    "UP",   # pyupgrade (outdated syntax for target Python version)
    "C4",   # flake8-comprehensions (unnecessary list/dict calls around generators)
    "PERF", # perflint (performance anti-patterns: unnecessary list() in for loops, etc.)
]

[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]
# Tests: allow unused args (fixtures), prints (debugging), commented code (examples)
"test_*.py" = ["ARG", "T20", "ERA"]
# Re-export modules: unused imports are intentional
"v7/hardware.py" = ["F401"]

file.content:
name: AERIS-10 CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]

jobs:
  # ===========================================================================
  # Python: lint (ruff), syntax check (py_compile), unit tests (pytest)
  # CI structure proposed by hcm444 — uses uv for dependency management
  # ===========================================================================
  python-tests:
    name: Python Lint + Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: uv sync --group dev

      - name: Ruff lint (whole repo)
        run: uv run ruff check .

      - name: Syntax check (py_compile)
        run: |
          uv run python - <<'PY'
          import py_compile
          from pathlib import Path

          skip = {".git", "__pycache__", ".venv", "venv", "docs"}
          for p in Path(".").rglob("*.py"):
              if skip & set(p.parts):
                  continue
              py_compile.compile(str(p), doraise=True)
          PY

      - name: Unit tests
        run: >
          uv run pytest
          9_Firmware/9_3_GUI/test_GUI_V65_Tk.py
          9_Firmware/9_3_GUI/test_v7.py
          -v --tb=short

  # ===========================================================================
  # MCU Firmware Unit Tests (20 tests)
  # Bug regression (15) + Gap-3 safety tests (5)
  # ===========================================================================
  mcu-tests:
    name: MCU Firmware Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install build tools
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y build-essential

      - name: Build and run MCU tests
        run: make test
        working-directory: 9_Firmware/9_1_Microcontroller/tests

  # ===========================================================================
  # FPGA RTL Regression (25 testbenches + lint)
  # ===========================================================================
  fpga-regression:
    name: FPGA Regression
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Icarus Verilog
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y iverilog

      - name: Run full FPGA regression
        run: bash run_regression.sh
        working-directory: 9_Firmware/9_2_FPGA

  # ===========================================================================
  # Cross-Layer Contract Tests (Python ↔ Verilog ↔ C)
  # Validates opcode maps, bit widths, packet layouts, and round-trip
  # correctness across FPGA RTL, Python GUI, and STM32 firmware.
  # ===========================================================================
  cross-layer-tests:
    name: Cross-Layer Contract Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: uv sync --group dev

      - name: Install Icarus Verilog
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y iverilog

      - name: Run cross-layer contract tests
        run: >
          uv run pytest
          9_Firmware/tests/cross_layer/test_cross_layer_contract.py
          -v --tb=short

1	`timescale 1ns / 1ps
2	
3	/**
4	 * radar_mode_controller.v
5	 *
6	 * Generates beam scanning and chirp mode control signals for the AERIS-10
7	 * receiver processing chain. This module drives:
8	 *   - use_long_chirp   : selects long (30us) or short (0.5us) chirp mode
9	 *   - mc_new_chirp     : toggle signal indicating new chirp start
10	 *   - mc_new_elevation : toggle signal indicating elevation step
11	 *   - mc_new_azimuth   : toggle signal indicating azimuth step
12	 *
13	 * These signals are consumed by matched_filter_multi_segment and
14	 * chirp_memory_loader_param in the receiver path.
15	 *
16	 * The controller mirrors the transmitter's chirp sequence defined in
17	 * plfm_chirp_controller_enhanced:
18	 *   - 32 chirps per elevation
19	 *   - 31 elevations per azimuth
20	 *   - 50 azimuths per full scan
21	 *   - Each chirp: Long chirp → Listen → Guard → Short chirp → Listen
22	 *
23	 * Modes of operation:
24	 *   mode[1:0]:
25	 *     2'b00 = STM32-driven (pass through stm32 toggle signals)
26	 *     2'b01 = Free-running auto-scan (internal timing)
27	 *     2'b10 = Single-chirp (fire one chirp per trigger, for debug)
28	 *     2'b11 = Reserved
29	 *
30	 * Clock domain: clk (100 MHz)
31	 */
32	
33	module radar_mode_controller #(
34	    parameter CHIRPS_PER_ELEVATION = 32,
35	    parameter ELEVATIONS_PER_AZIMUTH = 31,
36	    parameter AZIMUTHS_PER_SCAN = 50,
37	
38	    // Timing in 100 MHz clock cycles
39	    // Long chirp: 30us = 3000 cycles at 100 MHz
40	    // Long listen: 137us = 13700 cycles
41	    // Guard: 175.4us = 17540 cycles
42	    // Short chirp: 0.5us = 50 cycles
43	    // Short listen: 174.5us = 17450 cycles
44	    parameter LONG_CHIRP_CYCLES   = 3000,
45	    parameter LONG_LISTEN_CYCLES  = 13700,
46	    parameter GUARD_CYCLES        = 17540,
47	    parameter SHORT_CHIRP_CYCLES  = 50,
48	    parameter SHORT_LISTEN_CYCLES = 17450
49	) (
50	    input wire clk,
51	    input wire reset_n,
52	
53	    // Mode selection
54	    input wire [1:0] mode,          // 00=STM32, 01=auto, 10=single, 11=rsvd
55	
56	    // STM32 pass-through inputs (active in mode 00)
57	    input wire stm32_new_chirp,
58	    input wire stm32_new_elevation,
59	    input wire stm32_new_azimuth,
60	
61	    // Single-chirp trigger (active in mode 10)
62	    input wire trigger,
63	
64	    // Gap 2: Runtime-configurable timing inputs from host USB commands.
65	    // When connected, these override the compile-time parameters.
66	    // When left at default (tied to parameter values at instantiation),
67	    // behavior is identical to pre-Gap-2.
68	    input wire [15:0] cfg_long_chirp_cycles,
69	    input wire [15:0] cfg_long_listen_cycles,
70	    input wire [15:0] cfg_guard_cycles,
71	    input wire [15:0] cfg_short_chirp_cycles,
72	    input wire [15:0] cfg_short_listen_cycles,
73	    input wire [5:0]  cfg_chirps_per_elev,
74	
75	    // Outputs to receiver processing chain
76	    output reg use_long_chirp,
77	    output reg mc_new_chirp,
78	    output reg mc_new_elevation,
79	    output reg mc_new_azimuth,
80	
81	    // Beam position tracking
82	    output reg [5:0] chirp_count,
83	    output reg [5:0] elevation_count,
84	    output reg [5:0] azimuth_count,
85	
86	    // Status
87	    output wire scanning,       // 1 = scan in progress
88	    output wire scan_complete   // pulse when full scan done
89	
90	`ifdef FORMAL
91	    ,
92	    output wire [2:0]  fv_scan_state,
93	    output wire [17:0] fv_timer
94	`endif
95	);
96	
97	// ============================================================================
98	// INTERNAL STATE
99	// ============================================================================
100	
101	// Auto-scan state machine
102	reg [2:0] scan_state;
103	localparam S_IDLE        = 3'd0;
104	localparam S_LONG_CHIRP  = 3'd1;
105	localparam S_LONG_LISTEN = 3'd2;
106	localparam S_GUARD       = 3'd3;
107	localparam S_SHORT_CHIRP = 3'd4;
108	localparam S_SHORT_LISTEN = 3'd5;
109	localparam S_ADVANCE     = 3'd6;
110	
111	// Timing counter
112	reg [17:0] timer;  // enough for up to 262143 cycles (~2.6ms at 100 MHz)
113	
114	`ifdef FORMAL
115	assign fv_scan_state = scan_state;
116	assign fv_timer      = timer;
117	`endif
118	
119	// Edge detection for STM32 pass-through
120	reg stm32_new_chirp_prev;
121	reg stm32_new_elevation_prev;
122	reg stm32_new_azimuth_prev;
123	
124	// Trigger edge detection (for single-chirp mode)
125	reg trigger_prev;
126	wire trigger_pulse = trigger & ~trigger_prev;
127	
128	// Scan completion
129	reg scan_done_pulse;
130	
131	// ============================================================================
132	// EDGE DETECTION
133	// ============================================================================
134	always @(posedge clk or negedge reset_n) begin
135	    if (!reset_n) begin
136	        stm32_new_chirp_prev     <= 1'b0;
137	        stm32_new_elevation_prev <= 1'b0;
138	        stm32_new_azimuth_prev   <= 1'b0;
139	        trigger_prev             <= 1'b0;
140	    end else begin
141	        stm32_new_chirp_prev     <= stm32_new_chirp;
142	        stm32_new_elevation_prev <= stm32_new_elevation;
143	        stm32_new_azimuth_prev   <= stm32_new_azimuth;
144	        trigger_prev             <= trigger;
145	    end
146	end
147	
148	wire stm32_chirp_toggle     = stm32_new_chirp     ^ stm32_new_chirp_prev;
149	wire stm32_elevation_toggle = stm32_new_elevation  ^ stm32_new_elevation_prev;
150	wire stm32_azimuth_toggle   = stm32_new_azimuth    ^ stm32_new_azimuth_prev;
151	
152	// ============================================================================
153	// MAIN STATE MACHINE
154	// ============================================================================
155	always @(posedge clk or negedge reset_n) begin
156	    if (!reset_n) begin
157	        scan_state      <= S_IDLE;
158	        timer           <= 18'd0;
159	        use_long_chirp  <= 1'b1;
160	        mc_new_chirp    <= 1'b0;
161	        mc_new_elevation <= 1'b0;
162	        mc_new_azimuth  <= 1'b0;
163	        chirp_count     <= 6'd0;
164	        elevation_count <= 6'd0;
165	        azimuth_count   <= 6'd0;
166	        scan_done_pulse <= 1'b0;
167	    end else begin
168	        // Clear one-shot signals
169	        scan_done_pulse <= 1'b0;
170	
171	        case (mode)
172	        // ================================================================
173	        // MODE 00: STM32-driven pass-through
174	        // The STM32 firmware controls timing; we just detect toggle edges
175	        // and forward them to the receiver chain.
176	        // ================================================================
177	        2'b00: begin
178	            // Reset auto-scan state
179	            scan_state <= S_IDLE;
180	            timer      <= 18'd0;
181	
182	            // Pass through toggle signals
183	            if (stm32_chirp_toggle) begin
184	                mc_new_chirp <= ~mc_new_chirp;  // Toggle output
185	                use_long_chirp <= 1'b1;         // Default to long chirp
186	
187	                // Track chirp count (Gap 2: use runtime cfg_chirps_per_elev)
188	                if (chirp_count < cfg_chirps_per_elev - 1)
189	                    chirp_count <= chirp_count + 1;
190	                else
191	                    chirp_count <= 6'd0;
192	            end
193	
194	            if (stm32_elevation_toggle) begin
195	                mc_new_elevation <= ~mc_new_elevation;
196	                chirp_count <= 6'd0;
197	
198	                if (elevation_count < ELEVATIONS_PER_AZIMUTH - 1)
199	                    elevation_count <= elevation_count + 1;
200	                else
201	                    elevation_count <= 6'd0;
202	            end
203	
204	            if (stm32_azimuth_toggle) begin
205	                mc_new_azimuth <= ~mc_new_azimuth;
206	                elevation_count <= 6'd0;
207	
208	                if (azimuth_count < AZIMUTHS_PER_SCAN - 1)
209	                    azimuth_count <= azimuth_count + 1;
210	                else begin
211	                    azimuth_count <= 6'd0;
212	                    scan_done_pulse <= 1'b1;
213	                end
214	            end
215	        end
216	
217	        // ================================================================
218	        // MODE 01: Free-running auto-scan
219	        // Internally generates chirp timing matching the transmitter.
220	        // ================================================================
221	        2'b01: begin
222	            case (scan_state)
223	            S_IDLE: begin
224	                // Start first chirp immediately
225	                scan_state     <= S_LONG_CHIRP;
226	                timer          <= 18'd0;
227	                use_long_chirp <= 1'b1;
228	                mc_new_chirp   <= ~mc_new_chirp;  // Toggle to start chirp
229	                chirp_count    <= 6'd0;
230	                elevation_count <= 6'd0;
231	                azimuth_count  <= 6'd0;
232	
233	                `ifdef SIMULATION
234	                $display("[MODE_CTRL] Auto-scan starting");
235	                `endif
236	            end
237	
238	            S_LONG_CHIRP: begin
239	                use_long_chirp <= 1'b1;
240	                if (timer < cfg_long_chirp_cycles - 1)
241	                    timer <= timer + 1;
242	                else begin
243	                    timer <= 18'd0;
244	                    scan_state <= S_LONG_LISTEN;
245	                end
246	            end
247	
248	            S_LONG_LISTEN: begin
249	                if (timer < cfg_long_listen_cycles - 1)
250	                    timer <= timer + 1;
251	                else begin
252	                    timer <= 18'd0;
253	                    scan_state <= S_GUARD;
254	                end
255	            end
256	
257	            S_GUARD: begin
258	                if (timer < cfg_guard_cycles - 1)
259	                    timer <= timer + 1;
260	                else begin
261	                    timer <= 18'd0;
262	                    scan_state <= S_SHORT_CHIRP;
263	                    use_long_chirp <= 1'b0;
264	                end
265	            end
266	
267	            S_SHORT_CHIRP: begin
268	                use_long_chirp <= 1'b0;
269	                if (timer < cfg_short_chirp_cycles - 1)
270	                    timer <= timer + 1;
271	                else begin
272	                    timer <= 18'd0;
273	                    scan_state <= S_SHORT_LISTEN;
274	                end
275	            end
276	
277	            S_SHORT_LISTEN: begin
278	                if (timer < cfg_short_listen_cycles - 1)
279	                    timer <= timer + 1;
280	                else begin
281	                    timer <= 18'd0;
282	                    scan_state <= S_ADVANCE;
283	                end
284	            end
285	
286	            S_ADVANCE: begin
287	                // Advance chirp/elevation/azimuth counters
288	                // (Gap 2: use runtime cfg_chirps_per_elev)
289	                if (chirp_count < cfg_chirps_per_elev - 1) begin
290	                    // Next chirp in current elevation
291	                    chirp_count  <= chirp_count + 1;
292	                    mc_new_chirp <= ~mc_new_chirp;
293	                    scan_state   <= S_LONG_CHIRP;
294	                    use_long_chirp <= 1'b1;
295	                end else begin
296	                    chirp_count <= 6'd0;
297	
298	                    if (elevation_count < ELEVATIONS_PER_AZIMUTH - 1) begin
299	                        // Next elevation
300	                        elevation_count  <= elevation_count + 1;
301	                        mc_new_chirp     <= ~mc_new_chirp;
302	                        mc_new_elevation <= ~mc_new_elevation;
303	                        scan_state       <= S_LONG_CHIRP;
304	                        use_long_chirp   <= 1'b1;
305	                    end else begin
306	                        elevation_count <= 6'd0;
307	
308	                        if (azimuth_count < AZIMUTHS_PER_SCAN - 1) begin
309	                            // Next azimuth
310	                            azimuth_count    <= azimuth_count + 1;
311	                            mc_new_chirp     <= ~mc_new_chirp;
312	                            mc_new_elevation <= ~mc_new_elevation;
313	                            mc_new_azimuth   <= ~mc_new_azimuth;
314	                            scan_state       <= S_LONG_CHIRP;
315	                            use_long_chirp   <= 1'b1;
316	                        end else begin
317	                            // Full scan complete — restart
318	                            azimuth_count   <= 6'd0;
319	                            scan_done_pulse <= 1'b1;
320	                            mc_new_chirp    <= ~mc_new_chirp;
321	                            mc_new_elevation <= ~mc_new_elevation;
322	                            mc_new_azimuth  <= ~mc_new_azimuth;
323	                            scan_state      <= S_LONG_CHIRP;
324	                            use_long_chirp  <= 1'b1;
325	
326	                            `ifdef SIMULATION
327	                            $display("[MODE_CTRL] Full scan complete, restarting");
328	                            `endif
329	                        end
330	                    end
331	                end
332	            end
333	
334	            default: scan_state <= S_IDLE;
335	            endcase
336	        end
337	
338	        // ================================================================
339	        // MODE 10: Single-chirp (debug mode)
340	        // Fire one long chirp per trigger pulse, no scanning.
341	        // ================================================================
342	        2'b10: begin
343	            case (scan_state)
344	            S_IDLE: begin
345	                if (trigger_pulse) begin
346	                    scan_state     <= S_LONG_CHIRP;
347	                    timer          <= 18'd0;
348	                    use_long_chirp <= 1'b1;
349	                    mc_new_chirp   <= ~mc_new_chirp;
350	                end
351	            end
352	
353	            S_LONG_CHIRP: begin
354	                if (timer < cfg_long_chirp_cycles - 1)
355	                    timer <= timer + 1;
356	                else begin
357	                    timer <= 18'd0;
358	                    scan_state <= S_LONG_LISTEN;
359	                end
360	            end
361	
362	            S_LONG_LISTEN: begin
363	                if (timer < cfg_long_listen_cycles - 1)
364	                    timer <= timer + 1;
365	                else begin
366	                    // Single chirp done, return to idle
367	                    timer      <= 18'd0;
368	                    scan_state <= S_IDLE;
369	                end
370	            end
371	
372	            default: scan_state <= S_IDLE;
373	            endcase
374	        end
375	
376	        // ================================================================
377	        // MODE 11: Reserved — idle
378	        // ================================================================
379	        2'b11: begin
380	            scan_state <= S_IDLE;
381	            timer      <= 18'd0;
382	        end
383	
384	        endcase
385	    end
386	end
387	
388	// ============================================================================
389	// OUTPUT ASSIGNMENTS
390	// ============================================================================
391	assign scanning      = (scan_state != S_IDLE);
392	assign scan_complete = scan_done_pulse;
393	
394	endmodule
395

content.content:
1	`timescale 1ns / 1ps
2	
3	/**
4	 * radar_system_top.v
5	 * 
6	 * Complete Radar System Top Module
7	 * Integrates:
8	 * - Radar Transmitter (PLFM chirp generation)
9	 * - Radar Receiver (ADC interface, DDC, matched filtering, Doppler processing)
10	 * - USB Data Interface (FT601 USB 3.0 or FT2232H USB 2.0, selected by USB_MODE)
11	 * 
12	 * Clock domains:
13	 * - clk_100m: System clock (100MHz)
14	 * - clk_120m_dac: DAC clock (120MHz)
15	 * - ft601_clk: USB interface clock (100MHz FT601 or 60MHz FT2232H)
16	 *
17	 * USB_MODE parameter:
18	 *   0 = FT601 (32-bit, USB 3.0) — 200T premium board
19	 *   1 = FT2232H (8-bit, USB 2.0) — 50T production board
20	 */
21	
22	module radar_system_top (
23	    // System Clocks
24	    input wire clk_100m,                // 100MHz system clock
25	    input wire clk_120m_dac,             // 120MHz DAC clock
26	    input wire ft601_clk_in,             // FT601 clock (100MHz)
27	    input wire reset_n,                   // Active-low reset
28	    
29	    // ========== TRANSMITTER INTERFACES ==========
30	    
31	    // DAC Interface
32	    output wire [7:0] dac_data,
33	    output wire dac_clk,
34	    output wire dac_sleep,
35	    
36	    // RF Switch Control
37	    output wire fpga_rf_switch,
38	    
39	    // Mixer Enables
40	    output wire rx_mixer_en,
41	    output wire tx_mixer_en,
42	    
43	    // ADAR1000 Beamformer Control (via level shifters)
44	    output wire adar_tx_load_1, adar_rx_load_1,
45	    output wire adar_tx_load_2, adar_rx_load_2,
46	    output wire adar_tx_load_3, adar_rx_load_3,
47	    output wire adar_tx_load_4, adar_rx_load_4,
48	    output wire adar_tr_1, adar_tr_2, adar_tr_3, adar_tr_4,
49	    
50	    // Level Shifter SPI Interface (STM32F7 to ADAR1000)
51	    input wire stm32_sclk_3v3,
52	    input wire stm32_mosi_3v3,
53	    output wire stm32_miso_3v3,
54	    input wire stm32_cs_adar1_3v3, stm32_cs_adar2_3v3, 
55	    input wire stm32_cs_adar3_3v3, stm32_cs_adar4_3v3,
56	    
57	    output wire stm32_sclk_1v8,
58	    output wire stm32_mosi_1v8,
59	    input wire stm32_miso_1v8,
60	    output wire stm32_cs_adar1_1v8, stm32_cs_adar2_1v8,
61	    output wire stm32_cs_adar3_1v8, stm32_cs_adar4_1v8,
62	    
63	    // ========== RECEIVER INTERFACES ==========
64	    
65	    // ADC Physical Interface (LVDS)
66	    input wire [7:0] adc_d_p,            // ADC Data P (LVDS)
67	    input wire [7:0] adc_d_n,            // ADC Data N (LVDS)
68	    input wire adc_dco_p,                 // Data Clock Output P (400MHz LVDS)
69	    input wire adc_dco_n,                 // Data Clock Output N (400MHz LVDS)
70	    output wire adc_pwdn,                  // ADC Power Down
71	    
72	    // ========== STM32 CONTROL INTERFACES ==========
73	    
74	    // Chirp/Beam Control (toggle signals from STM32)
75	    input wire stm32_new_chirp,
76	    input wire stm32_new_elevation,
77	    input wire stm32_new_azimuth,
78	    input wire stm32_mixers_enable,
79	    
80	    // ========== FT601 USB 3.0 INTERFACE ==========
81	    
82	    // Data bus
83	    inout wire [31:0] ft601_data,         // 32-bit bidirectional data bus
84	    output wire [3:0] ft601_be,            // Byte enable (4 lanes for 32-bit mode)
85	    
86	    // Control signals
87	    output wire ft601_txe_n,                // Transmit enable (active low)
88	    output wire ft601_rxf_n,                // Receive enable (active low)
89	    input wire ft601_txe,                    // Transmit FIFO empty
90	    input wire ft601_rxf,                    // Receive FIFO full
91	    output wire ft601_wr_n,                  // Write strobe (active low)
92	    output wire ft601_rd_n,                  // Read strobe (active low)
93	    output wire ft601_oe_n,                  // Output enable (active low)
94	    output wire ft601_siwu_n,                 // Send immediate / Wakeup
95	    
96	    // FIFO flags
97	    input wire [1:0] ft601_srb,              // Selected read buffer
98	    input wire [1:0] ft601_swb,               // Selected write buffer
99	    
100	    // Clock output (optional, FT601 only — not used for FT2232H)
101	    output wire ft601_clk_out,
102	    
103	    // ========== FT2232H USB 2.0 INTERFACE (USB_MODE=1) ==========
104	    // 8-bit bidirectional data bus (245 Synchronous FIFO mode, Channel A)
105	    inout wire [7:0] ft_data,            // 8-bit bidirectional data bus
106	    input wire ft_rxf_n,                  // RX FIFO not empty (active low)
107	    input wire ft_txe_n,                  // TX FIFO not full (active low)
108	    output wire ft_rd_n,                  // Read strobe (active low)
109	    output wire ft_wr_n,                  // Write strobe (active low)
110	    output wire ft_oe_n,                  // Output enable / bus direction
111	    output wire ft_siwu,                  // Send Immediate / WakeUp
112	
113	    // ========== STATUS OUTPUTS ==========
114	    
115	    // Beam position tracking
116	    output wire [5:0] current_elevation,
117	    output wire [5:0] current_azimuth,
118	    output wire [5:0] current_chirp,
119	    output wire new_chirp_frame,
120	    
121	    // Doppler processing outputs (for debugging)
122	    output wire [31:0] dbg_doppler_data,
123	    output wire dbg_doppler_valid,
124	    output wire [4:0] dbg_doppler_bin,
125	    output wire [5:0] dbg_range_bin,
126	    
127	    // System status
128	    output wire [3:0] system_status,
129	
130	    // FPGA→STM32 GPIO outputs (DIG_5..DIG_7 on 50T board)
131	    // Used by STM32 outer AGC loop to read saturation state without USB polling.
132	    output wire gpio_dig5,          // DIG_5 (H11→PD13): AGC saturation flag (1=clipping detected)
133	    output wire gpio_dig6,          // DIG_6 (G12→PD14): AGC enable flag (mirrors host_agc_enable)
134	    output wire gpio_dig7           // DIG_7 (H12→PD15): reserved (tied low)
135	);
136	
137	// ============================================================================
138	// PARAMETERS
139	// ============================================================================
140	
141	// System configuration
142	parameter USE_LONG_CHIRP = 1'b1;          // Default to long chirp
143	parameter DOPPLER_ENABLE = 1'b1;           // Enable Doppler processing
144	parameter USB_ENABLE = 1'b1;               // Enable USB data transfer
145	parameter USB_MODE = 1;                    // 0=FT601 (32-bit, 200T), 1=FT2232H (8-bit, 50T production default)
146	
147	// ============================================================================
148	// INTERNAL SIGNALS
149	// ============================================================================
150	
151	// Clock and reset
152	wire clk_100m_buf;
153	wire clk_120m_dac_buf;
154	wire ft601_clk_buf;
155	wire sys_reset_n;
156	wire sys_reset_120m_n;  // Reset synchronized to clk_120m_dac domain
157	wire sys_reset_ft601_n; // Reset synchronized to ft601_clk domain
158	
159	// CDC: synchronized versions of async inputs for status_reg
160	wire stm32_mixers_enable_100m;  // stm32_mixers_enable sync'd to clk_100m
161	wire ft601_txe_100m;            // ft601_txe sync'd to clk_100m
162	
163	// Transmitter internal signals
164	wire [7:0] tx_chirp_data;
165	wire tx_chirp_valid;
166	wire tx_chirp_done;
167	wire tx_new_chirp_frame;        // In clk_120m_dac domain
168	wire tx_new_chirp_frame_sync;   // Synchronized to clk_100m domain
169	wire [5:0] tx_current_elevation;
170	wire [5:0] tx_current_azimuth;
171	wire [5:0] tx_current_chirp;       // In clk_120m_dac domain
172	wire [5:0] tx_current_chirp_sync;  // Synchronized to clk_100m domain
173	wire tx_current_chirp_sync_valid;
174	
175	// Receiver internal signals
176	wire [31:0] rx_doppler_output;
177	wire rx_doppler_valid;
178	wire [4:0] rx_doppler_bin;
179	wire [5:0] rx_range_bin;
180	wire [31:0] rx_range_profile;
181	wire rx_range_valid;
182	wire [15:0] rx_doppler_real;
183	wire [15:0] rx_doppler_imag;
184	wire rx_doppler_data_valid;
185	reg rx_detect_flag;       // Threshold detection result (was rx_cfar_detection)
186	reg rx_detect_valid;      // Detection valid pulse (was rx_cfar_valid)
187	
188	// Frame-complete signal from Doppler processor (for CFAR)
189	wire rx_frame_complete;
190	
191	// ADC debug tap from receiver (clk_100m domain, post-DDC)
192	wire [15:0] rx_dbg_adc_i;
193	wire [15:0] rx_dbg_adc_q;
194	wire        rx_dbg_adc_valid;
195	
196	// AGC status from receiver (for status readback and GPIO)
197	wire [7:0]  rx_agc_saturation_count;
198	wire [7:0]  rx_agc_peak_magnitude;
199	wire [3:0]  rx_agc_current_gain;
200	
201	// Data packing for USB
202	wire [31:0] usb_range_profile;
203	wire usb_range_valid;
204	wire [15:0] usb_doppler_real;
205	wire [15:0] usb_doppler_imag;
206	wire usb_doppler_valid;
207	wire usb_detect_flag;     // (was usb_cfar_detection)
208	wire usb_detect_valid;    // (was usb_cfar_valid)
209	
210	// System status
211	reg [3:0] status_reg;
212	
213	// USB host command outputs (Gap 4: USB Read Path)
214	// These are in the ft601_clk domain; CDC'd to clk_100m below
215	wire [31:0] usb_cmd_data;
216	wire        usb_cmd_valid;     // 1-cycle pulse in ft601_clk domain
217	wire [7:0]  usb_cmd_opcode;
218	wire [7:0]  usb_cmd_addr;
219	wire [15:0] usb_cmd_value;

221	// USB command decode registers (clk_100m domain, driven by CDC block below)
222	// Declared here (before rx_inst) so Icarus Verilog can resolve forward refs.
223	reg [1:0]  host_radar_mode;
224	reg        host_trigger_pulse;
225	reg [15:0] host_detect_threshold;  // (was host_cfar_threshold)
226	reg [2:0]  host_stream_control;
227	
228	// Fix 3: Digital gain control register
229	// [3]=direction: 0=amplify, 1=attenuate. [2:0]=shift amount 0..7.
230	// Default 0x00 = pass-through (no gain change).
231	reg [3:0]  host_gain_shift;
232	
233	// Gap 2: Host-configurable chirp timing registers
234	// These override the compile-time defaults in radar_mode_controller when
235	// written via USB command. Defaults match the parameter values in
236	// radar_mode_controller.v so behavior is unchanged until the host writes them.
237	reg [15:0] host_long_chirp_cycles;    // Opcode 0x10 (default 3000)
238	reg [15:0] host_long_listen_cycles;   // Opcode 0x11 (default 13700)
239	reg [15:0] host_guard_cycles;         // Opcode 0x12 (default 17540)
240	reg [15:0] host_short_chirp_cycles;   // Opcode 0x13 (default 50)
241	reg [15:0] host_short_listen_cycles;  // Opcode 0x14 (default 17450)
242	reg [5:0]  host_chirps_per_elev;      // Opcode 0x15 (default 32)
243	reg        host_status_request;       // Opcode 0xFF (self-clearing pulse)
244	
245	// Fix 4: Doppler/chirps mismatch protection
246	// DOPPLER_FRAME_CHIRPS is the fixed chirp count expected by the staggered-PRI
247	// Doppler path (16 long + 16 short). If host sets chirps_per_elev to a
248	// different value, Doppler accumulation is corrupted. Clamp at command decode
249	// and flag the mismatch so the host knows.
250	localparam DOPPLER_FRAME_CHIRPS = 32; // Total chirps per Doppler frame
251	reg        chirps_mismatch_error;     // Set if host tried to set chirps != FFT size
252	
253	// Fix 7: Range-mode register (opcode 0x20)
254	// Future-proofing for 3km/10km antenna switching.
255	//   2'b00 = Auto (default — system selects based on scene)
256	//   2'b01 = Short-range (3km)
257	//   2'b10 = Long-range (10km)
258	//   2'b11 = Reserved
259	// Currently a configuration store only — antenna/timing switching TBD.
260	reg [1:0]  host_range_mode;
261	
262	// CFAR configuration registers (host-configurable via USB)
263	reg [3:0]  host_cfar_guard;       // Opcode 0x21: guard cells per side (0..8)
264	reg [4:0]  host_cfar_train;       // Opcode 0x22: training cells per side (1..16)
265	reg [7:0]  host_cfar_alpha;       // Opcode 0x23: threshold multiplier (Q4.4)
266	reg [1:0]  host_cfar_mode;        // Opcode 0x24: 00=CA, 01=GO, 10=SO
267	reg        host_cfar_enable;      // Opcode 0x25: 1=CFAR, 0=simple threshold
268	
269	// Ground clutter removal registers (host-configurable via USB)
270	reg        host_mti_enable;       // Opcode 0x26: 1=MTI active, 0=pass-through
271	reg [2:0]  host_dc_notch_width;   // Opcode 0x27: DC notch ±width bins (0=off, 1..7)
272	
273	// AGC configuration registers (host-configurable via USB, opcodes 0x28-0x2C)
274	reg        host_agc_enable;       // Opcode 0x28: 0=manual gain, 1=auto AGC
275	reg [7:0]  host_agc_target;       // Opcode 0x29: target peak magnitude (default 200)
276	reg [3:0]  host_agc_attack;       // Opcode 0x2A: gain-down step on clipping (default 1)
277	reg [3:0]  host_agc_decay;        // Opcode 0x2B: gain-up step when weak (default 1)
278	reg [3:0]  host_agc_holdoff;      // Opcode 0x2C: frames to wait before gain-up (default 4)
279	
280	// Board bring-up self-test registers (opcode 0x30 trigger, 0x31 readback)
281	reg        host_self_test_trigger;  // Opcode 0x30: self-clearing pulse
282	wire       self_test_busy;
283	wire       self_test_result_valid;
284	wire [4:0] self_test_result_flags;  // Per-test PASS(1)/FAIL(0)
285	wire [7:0] self_test_result_detail; // Diagnostic detail byte
286	// Self-test latched results (hold until next trigger)
287	reg  [4:0] self_test_flags_latched;
288	reg  [7:0] self_test_detail_latched;
289	// Self-test ADC capture wires
290	wire       self_test_capture_active;
291	wire [15:0] self_test_capture_data;
292	wire       self_test_capture_valid;

294	// ============================================================================
295	// CLOCK BUFFERING
296	// ============================================================================
297	
298	`ifdef SIMULATION
299	// In simulation (iverilog), BUFG is not available — pass-through assigns
300	assign clk_100m_buf     = clk_100m;
301	assign clk_120m_dac_buf = clk_120m_dac;
302	assign ft601_clk_buf    = ft601_clk_in;
303	`else
304	BUFG bufg_100m (
305	    .I(clk_100m),
306	    .O(clk_100m_buf)
307	);
308	
309	BUFG bufg_120m (
310	    .I(clk_120m_dac),
311	    .O(clk_120m_dac_buf)
312	);
313	
314	BUFG bufg_ft601 (
315	    .I(ft601_clk_in),
316	    .O(ft601_clk_buf)
317	);
318	`endif
319	
320	// Reset synchronization (clk_100m domain)
321	(* ASYNC_REG = "TRUE" *) reg [1:0] reset_sync;
322	always @(posedge clk_100m_buf or negedge reset_n) begin
323	    if (!reset_n) begin
324	        reset_sync <= 2'b00;
325	    end else begin
326	        reset_sync <= {reset_sync[0], 1'b1};
327	    end
328	end
329	assign sys_reset_n = reset_sync[1];
330	
331	// Reset synchronization (clk_120m_dac domain)
332	// Ensures reset deassertion is synchronous to the DAC clock,
333	// preventing recovery/removal timing violations on 120 MHz FFs.
334	(* ASYNC_REG = "TRUE" *) reg [1:0] reset_sync_120m;
335	always @(posedge clk_120m_dac_buf or negedge reset_n) begin
336	    if (!reset_n) begin
337	        reset_sync_120m <= 2'b00;
338	    end else begin
339	        reset_sync_120m <= {reset_sync_120m[0], 1'b1};
340	    end
341	end
342	assign sys_reset_120m_n = reset_sync_120m[1];
343	
344	// Reset synchronization (ft601_clk domain)
345	// FT601 has its own asynchronous clock from the USB controller.
346	// All FT601-domain registers need a properly synchronized reset.
347	(* ASYNC_REG = "TRUE" *) reg [2:0] reset_sync_ft601;  // 3-stage for better MTBF
348	always @(posedge ft601_clk_buf or negedge reset_n) begin
349	    if (!reset_n) begin
350	        reset_sync_ft601 <= 3'b000;
351	    end else begin
352	        reset_sync_ft601 <= {reset_sync_ft601[1:0], 1'b1};
353	    end
354	end
355	assign sys_reset_ft601_n = reset_sync_ft601[2];
356	
357	// CDC synchronizers for status_reg inputs (async -> clk_100m)
358	// stm32_mixers_enable is an async GPIO; ft601_txe is on ft601_clk domain
359	cdc_single_bit #(.STAGES(2)) cdc_mixers_en_status (
360	    .src_clk(clk_100m_buf),           // Pseudo-source for async GPIO
361	    .dst_clk(clk_100m_buf),
362	    .reset_n(sys_reset_n),
363	    .src_signal(stm32_mixers_enable),
364	    .dst_signal(stm32_mixers_enable_100m)
365	);
366	
367	cdc_single_bit #(.STAGES(2)) cdc_ft601_txe_status (
368	    .src_clk(ft601_clk_buf),
369	    .dst_clk(clk_100m_buf),
370	    .reset_n(sys_reset_n),
371	    .src_signal(ft601_txe),
372	    .dst_signal(ft601_txe_100m)
373	);
374	
375	// ============================================================================
376	// CLOCK DOMAIN CROSSING: TRANSMITTER (120 MHz) -> SYSTEM (100 MHz)
377	// ============================================================================
378	
379	// CDC for chirp_counter: 6-bit multi-bit Gray-code synchronizer
380	// Source domain is clk_120m_dac, so reset must be synchronized to that domain.
381	// The cdc_adc_to_processing module uses synchronous reset internally, so
382	// using sys_reset_120m_n (120m-synchronized) is correct for the source side.
383	// The destination side will sample it synchronously on dst_clk, which at worst
384	// delays reset deassertion by 1-2 cycles — acceptable for CDC reset.
385	cdc_adc_to_processing #(
386	    .WIDTH(6),
387	    .STAGES(3)
388	) cdc_chirp_counter (
389	    .src_clk(clk_120m_dac_buf),
390	    .dst_clk(clk_100m_buf),
391	    .src_reset_n(sys_reset_120m_n),
392	    .dst_reset_n(sys_reset_n),
393	    .src_data(tx_current_chirp),
394	    .src_valid(1'b1),           // Always valid — counter updates continuously
395	    .dst_data(tx_current_chirp_sync),
396	    .dst_valid(tx_current_chirp_sync_valid)
397	);
398	
399	// CDC for new_chirp_frame: toggle CDC (pulse on clk_120m -> pulse on clk_100m)
400	// new_chirp_frame is a 1-cycle pulse on clk_120m_dac. A level synchronizer
401	// at 100 MHz can miss it. Toggle CDC converts pulse -> level toggle,
402	// synchronizes the toggle, then detects edges to recover the pulse.
403	reg chirp_frame_toggle_120m;
404	always @(posedge clk_120m_dac_buf or negedge sys_reset_120m_n) begin
405	    if (!sys_reset_120m_n)
406	        chirp_frame_toggle_120m <= 1'b0;
407	    else if (tx_new_chirp_frame)
408	        chirp_frame_toggle_120m <= ~chirp_frame_toggle_120m;
409	end
410	
411	wire chirp_frame_toggle_100m;
412	cdc_single_bit #(
413	    .STAGES(3)
414	) cdc_new_chirp_frame (
415	    .src_clk(clk_120m_dac_buf),
416	    .dst_clk(clk_100m_buf),
417	    .reset_n(sys_reset_n),
418	    .src_signal(chirp_frame_toggle_120m),
419	    .dst_signal(chirp_frame_toggle_100m)
420	);
421	
422	reg chirp_frame_toggle_100m_prev;
423	always @(posedge clk_100m_buf or negedge sys_reset_n) begin
424	    if (!sys_reset_n)
425	        chirp_frame_toggle_100m_prev <= 1'b0;
426	    else
427	        chirp_frame_toggle_100m_prev <= chirp_frame_toggle_100m;
428	end
429	assign tx_new_chirp_frame_sync = chirp_frame_toggle_100m ^ chirp_frame_toggle_100m_prev;
430

521	    .doppler_bin(rx_doppler_bin),
522	    .range_bin(rx_range_bin),
523	    
524	    // Matched filter range profile (for USB)
525	    .range_profile_i_out(rx_range_profile[15:0]),
526	    .range_profile_q_out(rx_range_profile[31:16]),
527	    .range_profile_valid_out(rx_range_valid),
528	    
529	    // Host command inputs (Gap 4: USB Read Path)
530	    .host_mode(host_radar_mode),
531	    .host_trigger(host_trigger_pulse),
532	    // Gap 2: Host-configurable chirp timing
533	    .host_long_chirp_cycles(host_long_chirp_cycles),

535	    .host_guard_cycles(host_guard_cycles),

537	    .host_short_listen_cycles(host_short_listen_cycles),
538	    .host_chirps_per_elev(host_chirps_per_elev),
539	    // Fix 3: digital gain control
540	    .host_gain_shift(host_gain_shift),

542	    .host_agc_enable(host_agc_enable),
543	    .host_agc_target(host_agc_target),
544	    .host_agc_attack(host_agc_attack),
